preFix.title: Refurbishing a leather car seat
preFix.subtitle: Clean, fill, and re-colour cracked or faded leather upholstery
preFix.excerpt: How to refurbish a leather car seat without replacing it: deep-cleaning to remove old conditioner and contamination, applying a leather filler to cracks and worn patches, re-colouring with a flexible leather dye, and sealing with a topcoat to protect the finish.
preFix.sourceNotes: Practical Upholstery (C.H. Doust). Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

content.text: Leather car seats crack and fade primarily because the colour and protective finish coat wears away before the leather itself fails. Applying new colour and a
to prepared leather extends the seat's life significantly.
addresses cracks before colouring so the new finish does not simply sink into damaged areas.

Leather prep or degreaser solution. Leather filler. 400-grit abrasive paper on a sanding block. Water-based leather dye matched to the original seat colour (most leather repair kit suppliers offer a colour-match service from a photograph). A foam applicator sponge or soft-bristle brush for applying dye. A flexible clear sealer. Masking tape to protect plastic and stitching during application.
Step 1, clean and prepare the surface

Wipe the entire seat with leather prep solution on a lint-free cloth, rubbing in circular motions until no more contamination transfers to the cloth. Prep removes silicone-based conditioners and wax that would prevent the filler and dye from bonding. Allow the surface to dry for 30 minutes in a warm, dry environment before proceeding.
Step 2, fill cracks and worn areas

Apply leather filler to cracks and worn patches with a finger or palette knife, pressing it into the crack and wiping the excess flush. Allow to dry for 20 minutes, then smooth the filled area with 400-grit abrasive paper on the sanding block using light strokes. Apply a second coat of filler to any area that has sunk on drying, and sand again. The filled area should be level with the surrounding surface before colouring.

Apply the leather dye in thin coats with the sponge applicator, working with the grain of the leather. Allow each coat to dry for 20 minutes before applying the next. Build up three to five thin coats for an even, opaque result. Once the final dye coat is fully dry, apply two coats of the flexible colour sealer, allowing full drying between coats. The sealer coat is what protects the finish in use; do not omit it.

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
